food_subgroup: Noodle soups — Korean spicy ramyun with fermented vegetables and seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean kimchi ramyun. FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodle soup) used as closest parent; no specific Korean kimchi ramyun entry exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of spicy kimchi shin ramyun, approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, broth, kimchi, squid rings, rice cake, and cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Food Composition Table — Ramyun noodle soup with kimchi and seafood; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central component data for squid, rice cake, and cabbage
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NAAS), Republic of Korea; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact restaurant preparation. Values estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using Korean Food Composition Table (NAAS) entries for kimchi ramyun broth base, wheat noodles, kimchi, and USDA FoodData Central entries for squid (FDC ID 175159), rice cake (tteok, FDC ID 2003567), and napa cabbage. Serving size 500 g assumed for a standard restaurant bowl. Per-100 g values derived from the composite. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy kimchi shin ramyun. GI of 52 estimated from the closest analogous entry: wheat-based Asian noodle soup (GI ~52, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The presence of kimchi (fermented, high-acid) and rice cake (moderate GI ~55) is consistent with a blended GI in the low-to-borderline range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 49.0) / 100 ≈ 25.5, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 9.8 × 5 = 49.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~2400 mg per 500 g serving) from spicy broth, kimchi, and seasoning packets
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load (~2400 mg per serving, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal); if consumed, request reduced broth volume and avoid drinking the soup base.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~25.5 per serving) driven by wheat noodles and rice cake; fermented kimchi may attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware of the moderate glycemic load per serving (~25.5); portion control is advised, and the fermented kimchi component may provide modest benefit to postprandial glycaemia, but the rice cake and noodle content warrants monitoring of blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth and squid; squid provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids partially offsetting cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~2400 mg per serving) is the primary concern and warrants limiting consumption frequency; the squid component contributes beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, and saturated fat per serving is moderate (~3 g), making sodium management the priority intervention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Kimchi Shin Ramyun is a Korean-style spicy noodle soup dish served at The Noodle Place restaurant in Singapore. It is built on a fiery, gochugaru-based broth inspired by the iconic Shin Ramyun instant noodle brand, elevated with restaurant-quality additions including fermented kimchi (napa cabbage kimchi), tender squid rings, chewy Korean rice cakes (tteok), and fresh cabbage. The noodles are wheat-based ramyun noodles with a firm, springy texture that absorbs the intensely spiced, umami-rich broth. The flavour profile is dominated by deep spice heat, sour fermented notes from the kimchi, and a savoury seafood sweetness from the squid. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~360 kcal per 500 g serving), provides meaningful protein (~21 g per serving) from squid and noodles, and contains significant sodium (~2400 mg per serving) primarily from the spicy broth and kimchi. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at ~25.5 due to the noodle and rice cake carbohydrate content. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. The fermented kimchi component contributes probiotics and may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. This dish is not suitable for halal diets without verification, contains gluten and molluscs as allergens, and is not vegetarian or vegan.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact restaurant preparation of spicy kimchi shin ramyun. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using Korean Food Composition Table (NAAS) and USDA FoodData Central component data. GI was estimated from the closest analogous wheat noodle soup entry in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the Korean Food Composition Table or USDA component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the ingredient profile — wheat noodles, kimchi, squid, and rice cake do not contribute meaningful trans fat by formulation (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries). Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual restaurant portion may vary. Halal status is unverified — squid is generally permissible but kimchi seasoning and broth ingredients require restaurant-level verification. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 49.0) / 100 = 25.5, where 49.0 g carbohydrate = 9.8 g/100g × 500 g serving.
